network.overall_strategy: The network constructs DL-Proline via the experimentally validated cyanosulfidic pathway of Patel et al. (2015, Nature Chemistry), embedded within a broader prebiotic feedstock-generation framework spanning hydrothermal CO₂ fixation, Fischer-Tropsch-type synthesis, atmospheric photochemistry, and surface cyanosulfidic chemistry. The core 5-step cyanosulfidic route builds the pyrrolidine ring unique to proline through cyanohydrin formation, H₂S-mediated thiolation-cyclization, Cu/H₂S reductive deoxygenation, thione-to-nitrile exchange, and nitrile hydrolysis. Upstream hydrothermal reactions generate formaldehyde (via formate reduction), acetaldehyde (Fischer-Tropsch), and formamide, which feed into surface-environment aldol condensation, Michael addition, and formamide decomposition to supply the key starting materials HCN and 3-aminopropanal. Two independent HCN sources (formamide decomposition and atmospheric photochemistry) create convergent feedstock pathways. H₂S and NH₃ are catalytically recycled within the cyanosulfidic core. The product proline enters the prebiotic amino acid pool for peptide assembly in the biochemical environment. Note: the cyanosulfidic pathway produces racemic proline; the origin of L-homochirality remains an open question.

reactions.name: CO₂ reduction to formate on greigite

conditions.other: alkaline hydrothermal vent conditions, pH 9-11, H₂-rich fluids from serpentinization

reactions.mechanism: CO₂ is adsorbed onto the greigite (Fe₃S₄) surface, which is a structural analog of biological [Fe₄S₄] clusters. Molecular hydrogen dissociates on the iron-sulfide surface, generating surface-bound hydride. The hydride attacks the carbon of CO₂, reducing it to formate (HCOO⁻). The alkaline pH favors formate stability.
reactions.reasoning: Greigite-catalyzed CO₂ reduction to formate is experimentally demonstrated under simulated hydrothermal conditions (Roldan et al., 2015, Chemical Communications; Preiner et al., 2020, Nature Ecology & Evolution). This reaction is the first step in a Wood-Ljungdahl-like pathway on mineral surfaces and provides the C1 feedstock for downstream organic synthesis.

reactions.name: Formate reduction to formaldehyde on magnetite
reactions.mechanism: Formate is further reduced on the magnetite/mackinawite surface by dissociated H₂. The two-electron reduction of formate proceeds through a surface-bound formyl intermediate, yielding formaldehyde (HCHO) and water. The reaction is thermodynamically driven by the high H₂ fugacity in serpentinization-derived fluids.
reactions.reasoning: Further reduction of formate to formaldehyde on iron-oxide/iron-sulfide surfaces has been proposed as part of the stepwise CO₂ reduction cascade in hydrothermal systems (He et al., 2019, PNAS; Muchowska et al., 2017, JACS). While yields are low compared to formate, the continuous H₂ flux in serpentinizing systems can drive the equilibrium. Formaldehyde is a critical C1 building block for downstream aldol and Strecker chemistry.

reactions.name: Reverse water-gas shift on pyrite

conditions.other: black smoker vent conditions, high temperature zone

reactions.mechanism: CO₂ is reduced to CO via the reverse water-gas shift reaction on pyrite surfaces: CO₂ + H₂ → CO + H₂O. The FeS₂ surface provides the reducing power and catalytic sites for this well-known geochemical process at elevated temperatures.
reactions.reasoning: The reverse water-gas shift reaction on iron sulfide minerals at hydrothermal temperatures is thermodynamically favorable and experimentally documented (Huber & Wächtershäuser, 1997, Science; Cody et al., 2000, Science). CO produced serves as a feedstock for Fischer-Tropsch-type synthesis of C2+ organics.

reactions.name: Fischer-Tropsch-type synthesis of acetaldehyde

conditions.other: Fischer-Tropsch-type conditions on magnetite surface, subseafloor hydrothermal

reactions.mechanism: CO undergoes Fischer-Tropsch-type (FTT) reactions on magnetite surfaces with H₂. CO is adsorbed and hydrogenated through surface methylene/methyl intermediates. Chain growth to C2 produces acetaldehyde (CH₃CHO) as one of the oxygenated C2 products alongside methanol and ethanol.
reactions.reasoning: FTT synthesis of C2 oxygenated organics including acetaldehyde on magnetite and other iron-oxide/iron-sulfide catalysts under hydrothermal conditions is experimentally demonstrated (McCollom et al., 1999, Origins of Life; Rushdi & Simoneit, 2001, Origins of Life; Holm & Andersson, 2005). Acetaldehyde is a key building block for aldol condensation to longer-chain aldehydes.

reactions.name: Formamide synthesis from formate and ammonia

conditions.other: wet-dry cycling in evaporitic pools, dehydration conditions concentrate reactants and drive condensation

reactions.mechanism: Formate and ammonium ion are concentrated by evaporation in surface pools. Under dehydrating (dry phase) conditions on montmorillonite clay surfaces, amidation occurs: HCOO⁻ + NH₄⁺ → HCONH₂ + H₂O. The clay surface activates the carboxylate and provides a low-water microenvironment that drives the thermodynamically unfavorable condensation.
reactions.reasoning: Formamide synthesis from ammonium formate under dehydrating conditions is a well-established prebiotic route (Saladino et al., 2012, Chemistry & Biodiversity; Botta et al., 2018, European Journal of Organic Chemistry). Montmorillonite clays catalyze this condensation by concentrating reactants in interlayer spaces and lowering the activation energy. Formamide is a key precursor for HCN and nucleobase synthesis.

reactions.name: Formamide thermal/UV decomposition to HCN

conditions.other: UV irradiation (λ < 280 nm), no ozone shield on early Earth; dry phase in geothermal fields

reactions.mechanism: Formamide undergoes dehydration to HCN catalyzed by UV irradiation on TiO₂ photocatalytic surfaces. The UV photons excite TiO₂, generating reactive radical species that abstract the OH from formamide, or directly photolyze the C–O bond, producing HCN and H₂O. Mineral surfaces (clays, metal oxides) further promote the decomposition.
reactions.reasoning: Formamide decomposition to HCN on mineral surfaces including TiO₂ and montmorillonite is experimentally demonstrated (Saladino et al., 2001, Bioorganic & Medicinal Chemistry; Ferus et al., 2015, PNAS). UV-driven formamide chemistry is particularly efficient on anatase TiO₂, which is a common mineral on early Earth's surface. This provides a geochemically sourced HCN supply for cyanosulfidic chemistry.

reactions.name: Atmospheric photochemical HCN synthesis

conditions.other: Early Earth atmosphere with N₂ and CH₄ (or CO₂/N₂ with lightning); no ozone layer; HCN rains out into surface pools

reactions.mechanism: UV photolysis of atmospheric N₂ and CH₄ (and/or CO₂) produces HCN through radical recombination. N₂ is dissociated by extreme UV into N atoms, which react with CH₃ and CH₂ radicals (from CH₄ photolysis) to form HCN. Lightning and impact-generated plasma also contribute. HCN dissolves in rainwater and accumulates in surface pools, particularly in cold traps or evaporitic settings.
reactions.reasoning: Atmospheric photochemical production of HCN from N₂ and CH₄ (or N₂/CO₂) is a major prebiotic HCN source, extensively modeled and experimentally demonstrated (Zahnle, 1986, JGR; Tian et al., 2011, Earth and Planetary Science Letters; Rimmer & Rugheimer, 2019, Icarus). Estimated fluxes of 10⁸-10⁹ molecules cm⁻² s⁻¹ on the early Earth could sustain cyanosulfidic chemistry pools. TiO₂ mineral surfaces in the catchment area may assist photocatalytic activation.

reactions.name: Aldol condensation of formaldehyde and acetaldehyde to acrolein

conditions.other: mildly alkaline pH (8-10), surface pool near geothermal source receiving hydrothermal outflow

reactions.mechanism: Base-catalyzed aldol condensation followed by dehydration. The enolizable hydrogen of acetaldehyde is abstracted by basic mineral surfaces (clay, apatite), forming an enolate that attacks formaldehyde in a cross-aldol addition. The resulting β-hydroxyaldehyde (3-hydroxypropanal) spontaneously dehydrates under mild warming to yield acrolein (prop-2-enal, CH₂=CHCHO). Montmorillonite clay provides the basic catalytic sites and concentrates reactants through surface adsorption.
reactions.reasoning: Aldol condensation of small aldehydes on mineral surfaces is a well-established prebiotic transformation (Weber, 2001, Origins of Life; Orgel, 2004, Critical Reviews in Biochemistry; de Marcellus et al., 2015, PNAS). The specific cross-aldol of formaldehyde and acetaldehyde yielding acrolein is a textbook aldol-dehydration sequence favored by the greater electrophilicity of formaldehyde and the thermodynamic stability of the conjugated product. This provides the C3 α,β-unsaturated aldehyde needed for Michael addition with ammonia.

reactions.name: Michael addition of ammonia to acrolein yielding 3-aminopropanal

conditions.other: aqueous surface pool with dissolved NH₃ from volcanic outgassing; mildly basic pH; clay surface catalysis

reactions.mechanism: 1,4-Conjugate (Michael) addition of ammonia to acrolein. The nitrogen lone pair of NH₃ attacks the β-carbon of acrolein (the Michael acceptor), forming a C–N bond. Proton transfer yields 3-aminopropanal (NCCC=O). The reaction is kinetically facile in aqueous solution at ambient temperature. Montmorillonite clay concentrates both reactants through adsorption and may lower the activation barrier through surface acid-base catalysis.
reactions.reasoning: The Michael addition of ammonia to acrolein to produce 3-aminopropanal is the prebiotically plausible route to this key starting material identified by Patel et al. (2015, Nature Chemistry). The reaction is a straightforward conjugate addition that proceeds readily in aqueous solution. Patel et al. note that 3-aminopropanal is a product of cyanosulfidic/surface chemistry from simple C1-C3 feedstocks. Montmorillonite catalysis of amine-aldehyde chemistry is well documented (Bujdák & Rode, 1999, JME).

reactions.name: Strecker-type cyanohydrin formation (Step 1 of cyanosulfidic proline pathway)

conditions.other: cyanosulfidic surface pool conditions; mildly basic pH; HCN present from atmospheric/formamide sources

reactions.mechanism: Nucleophilic addition of cyanide (CN⁻) to the aldehyde carbonyl of 3-aminopropanal. The cyanide ion attacks the electrophilic carbonyl carbon, forming a new C–C bond and yielding the α-hydroxynitrile (cyanohydrin) 4-amino-2-hydroxybutanenitrile. The free primary amine group on the 3-aminopropanal chain is preserved for subsequent cyclization. This is a classic Strecker-type cyanohydrin formation step.
reactions.reasoning: This is Step 1 of the experimentally demonstrated cyanosulfidic proline pathway (Patel, Percivalle, Ritson, Duffy, and Sutherland, 2015, Nature Chemistry, DOI: 10.1038/nchem.2202). The Strecker-type cyanohydrin formation from aminoaldehyde + HCN is well-established prebiotic chemistry. The product 4-amino-2-hydroxybutanenitrile contains both the amine and nitrile functional groups required for subsequent H₂S-mediated ring closure.

reactions.name: H₂S-mediated thiolation and intramolecular cyclization (Step 2 of cyanosulfidic proline pathway)

conditions.other: cyanosulfidic conditions; H₂S atmosphere or dissolved H₂S from volcanic/hydrothermal sources

reactions.mechanism: H₂S attacks the nitrile carbon of 4-amino-2-hydroxybutanenitrile, converting C≡N to a thioamide (C=S). The terminal primary amine then performs intramolecular nucleophilic attack on the thioamide carbon, closing the five-membered pyrrolidine ring with release of NH₃. This one-pot thiolation-cyclization simultaneously achieves ring closure and introduces the thione functional group central to subsequent cyanosulfidic transformations.
reactions.reasoning: This is Step 2 of the experimentally demonstrated cyanosulfidic proline pathway (Patel et al., 2015, Nature Chemistry, DOI: 10.1038/nchem.2202). The H₂S-mediated activation of nitriles toward cyclization is a hallmark of the Powell-Sutherland cyanosulfidic framework. The intramolecular distance between the amine and thioamide in this substrate is exactly right for five-membered ring formation, explaining the selectivity for the pyrrolidine ring of proline.

reactions.name: Cu/H₂S reductive deoxygenation (Step 3 of cyanosulfidic proline pathway)

conditions.catalyst: elemental copper (Cu⁰) with H₂S as co-reductant
conditions.other: cyanosulfidic surface pool; native copper deposits formed by Cu²⁺ reduction by H₂S in sulfide-rich surface settings

reactions.mechanism: Reductive removal of the C3 hydroxyl group from 3-hydroxypyrrolidine-2-thione. The Cu/H₂S reducing system donates electrons to cleave the C–OH bond: Cu⁰ is oxidized (Cu⁰ → Cu⁺/Cu²⁺) while H₂S provides additional reducing equivalents and protons. The product is the fully reduced pyrrolidine-2-thione with loss of water. The thione group at C2 is retained.
reactions.reasoning: This is Step 3 of the experimentally demonstrated cyanosulfidic proline pathway (Patel et al., 2015, Nature Chemistry, DOI: 10.1038/nchem.2202). The Cu/H₂S reducing system is a prebiotically plausible reductant — native copper deposits form when dissolved Cu²⁺ ions are reduced by H₂S in geochemical settings. Patel et al. explicitly use elemental copper and H₂S as co-reductants for this transformation. The steady-state availability of Cu⁰ in prebiotic surface environments remains an open question (see Open Questions).

reactions.name: Thione-to-nitrile exchange with HCN (Step 4 of cyanosulfidic proline pathway)

conditions.other: cyanosulfidic conditions; HCN present; released H₂S is recycled into Step 2

reactions.mechanism: Cyanide displaces the thione sulfur at C2 of the pyrrolidine ring. HCN acts as a nucleophile, attacking the electrophilic thioamide carbon. The C=S bond is cleaved with release of H₂S, and a new C–CN bond is formed, yielding pyrrolidine-2-carbonitrile. This thione-to-nitrile exchange is a key interconversion in cyanosulfidic chemistry, converting sulfur-containing functional groups to nitrogen-containing ones. The released H₂S is available for recycling into the thiolation-cyclization step (rxn_011).
reactions.reasoning: This is Step 4 of the experimentally demonstrated cyanosulfidic proline pathway (Patel et al., 2015, Nature Chemistry, DOI: 10.1038/nchem.2202). The thione-to-nitrile exchange exemplifies the elegant sulfur cycling in cyanosulfidic chemistry: H₂S consumed in Step 2 (rxn_011) is regenerated here, creating a catalytic sulfur cycle. This HCN-driven displacement is thermodynamically favorable because the C≡N triple bond is more stable than the C=S double bond in the product context.

reactions.name: Nitrile hydrolysis to proline (Step 5 of cyanosulfidic proline pathway)

conditions.catalyst: phosphate minerals and clay surfaces may catalyze hydrolysis; metal ions (Fe²⁺, Zn²⁺) accelerate nitrile hydrolysis
conditions.other: 2 equivalents of H₂O consumed; proceeds through pyrrolidine-2-carboxamide intermediate; timescale of days to weeks at ambient temperature and neutral pH

reactions.mechanism: Sequential hydrolysis of the nitrile group of pyrrolidine-2-carbonitrile. Water attacks the nitrile carbon nucleophilically, forming a primary amide intermediate (pyrrolidine-2-carboxamide). The amide undergoes a second hydrolysis to the carboxylic acid with release of ammonia. Two equivalents of water are consumed overall. The product is racemic proline (DL-proline). Metal ions (Fe²⁺, Zn²⁺) present in surface pools and phosphate/clay mineral surfaces may catalyze the hydrolysis and accelerate the rate-limiting step.
reactions.reasoning: This is Step 5 of the experimentally demonstrated cyanosulfidic proline pathway (Patel et al., 2015, Nature Chemistry, DOI: 10.1038/nchem.2202). Nitrile hydrolysis is the final step completing the conversion from aminonitrile to amino acid. At ambient temperature and neutral pH, this step may be slow (days to weeks), constituting a potential kinetic bottleneck. However, the presence of dissolved metal ions (Fe²⁺, Zn²⁺, Cu²⁺) and mineral surfaces can significantly accelerate nitrile hydrolysis (Baxter et al., 2009, JOC). The pathway produces racemic proline; the origin of L-homochirality remains unresolved.

reactions.name: Proline-containing peptide formation via wet-dry cycling

conditions.other: wet-dry cycling in evaporitic pools; dehydration drives peptide bond condensation; repeated cycles increase oligopeptide length

reactions.mechanism: Proline and glycine are concentrated by evaporation on montmorillonite clay surfaces. During the dry (dehydrating) phase, the amino group of glycine attacks the activated carboxyl group of proline (or vice versa), forming a peptide bond with loss of water. Montmorillonite catalyzes the condensation by adsorbing amino acids in an orientation favorable for peptide bond formation. The cyclic pyrrolidine structure of proline introduces conformational rigidity (kinks and turns) into the resulting peptides, which may have functional significance in early protometabolism.
reactions.reasoning: Peptide bond formation via wet-dry cycling on clay surfaces is a well-established prebiotic polymerization mechanism (Lahav et al., 1978, Science; Rode, 1999, Peptides; Frenkel-Pinter et al., 2020, Chemical Reviews). Montmorillonite has been shown to catalyze the formation of peptides up to 50+ residues (Ferris et al., 1996, Nature). Proline-containing peptides are of particular interest because proline's rigid pyrrolidine ring constrains polypeptide backbone geometry, potentially generating early catalytic structures. Glycine is used as the co-reactant as it is the most abundant prebiotic amino acid (Miller, 1953).

pathways.name: Full hydrothermal-to-surface cyanosulfidic proline synthesis (formamide-derived HCN)
pathways.description: Complete route from CO₂ fixation in hydrothermal vents through formate → formamide → HCN in surface pools, combined with formaldehyde + acetaldehyde → acrolein → 3-aminopropanal, converging in the 5-step cyanosulfidic proline pathway. HCN from formamide decomposition drives both the cyanohydrin step and the thione-to-nitrile exchange.

pathways.name: Atmospheric HCN route to proline via cyanosulfidic core
pathways.description: Atmospheric photochemical HCN (from N₂ + CH₄ + UV) supplies the cyanide for both the cyanohydrin formation and thione-to-nitrile exchange steps of the cyanosulfidic proline pathway. 3-Aminopropanal is produced via the hydrothermal formaldehyde + acetaldehyde → acrolein → Michael addition route.

pathways.name: Formamide HCN branch for cyanohydrin step
pathways.description: HCN derived from the hydrothermal formate → surface formamide → HCN route feeds specifically into the cyanohydrin formation step (rxn_010), extending the 3-aminopropanal carbon chain to form 4-amino-2-hydroxybutanenitrile. This is the carbon-nitrogen bond-forming step that sets up the C4 chain for pyrrolidine ring closure.

pathways.name: Core cyanosulfidic proline pathway (Patel et al., 2015)
pathways.description: The experimentally validated 5-step cyanosulfidic pathway from 3-aminopropanal and HCN to proline: (1) cyanohydrin formation, (2) H₂S thiolation-cyclization, (3) Cu/H₂S reductive deoxygenation, (4) thione-to-nitrile exchange, (5) nitrile hydrolysis. This is the core of the network, constructing the pyrrolidine ring unique to proline through sulfur-mediated ring closure.

pathways.name: Atmospheric HCN branch for thione-to-nitrile exchange
pathways.description: Atmospheric photochemical HCN feeds specifically into the thione-to-nitrile exchange step (rxn_013), replacing the thione sulfur on pyrrolidine-2-thione with a nitrile group and regenerating H₂S for the sulfur cycle. This provides an independent HCN source for the final ring-modification steps.

pathways.name: H₂S catalytic sulfur cycle
pathways.description: Catalytic recycling of H₂S within the cyanosulfidic core: H₂S is consumed in the thiolation-cyclization step (rxn_011) and in the Cu/H₂S reductive deoxygenation (rxn_012), then regenerated by thione-to-nitrile exchange (rxn_013). This elegant sulfur cycle means that sub-stoichiometric H₂S can drive the overall transformation, with the sulfur serving as a catalytic shuttle between thioamide and nitrile functional groups.

pathways.name: NH₃ nitrogen recycling pathway
pathways.description: Ammonia is consumed upstream in formamide synthesis (rxn_005) and Michael addition to acrolein (rxn_009), and is regenerated by the thiolation-cyclization (rxn_011, which releases NH₃ during ring closure) and the final nitrile hydrolysis (rxn_014). This nitrogen economy means NH₃ functions as both a substrate and a recycled product, sustaining the network with sub-stoichiometric external nitrogen input.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal CO₂-to-formaldehyde feedstock route
pathways.description: Two-step hydrothermal reduction of CO₂ to formaldehyde via formate intermediate on greigite and magnetite mineral surfaces. Formaldehyde exits the hydrothermal system to surface pools where it serves as a C1 building block for acrolein synthesis via aldol condensation with acetaldehyde.

pathways.name: Fischer-Tropsch acetaldehyde route to 3-aminopropanal
pathways.description: CO₂ is first reduced to CO by the reverse water-gas shift on pyrite (rxn_003), then CO undergoes Fischer-Tropsch-type synthesis on magnetite to produce acetaldehyde (rxn_004). Acetaldehyde is transported to surface pools where it reacts with hydrothermal-derived formaldehyde in aldol condensation to form acrolein, which undergoes Michael addition with NH₃ to yield 3-aminopropanal, the starting substrate for the cyanosulfidic core.

pathways.name: Proline to peptide assembly in biochemical environment
pathways.description: Proline produced by the cyanosulfidic pathway enters the prebiotic amino acid pool and is incorporated into peptides via wet-dry cycling on montmorillonite clay surfaces. The rigid pyrrolidine ring of proline introduces conformational constraints (kinks, turns) into peptides, potentially generating early catalytic or structural motifs. This pathway connects surface cyanosulfidic chemistry to the biochemical assembly of macromolecules.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Comprehensive 13-step pathway tracing CO₂ all the way to DL-Proline through hydrothermal feedstock generation, formamide-derived HCN, and the experimentally validated cyanosulfidic core. The upstream hydrothermal steps (rxn_001–004) are plausible but rxn_002 (formate → formaldehyde on magnetite) is the weakest link, with limited direct experimental support at the yields needed. The aldol condensation (rxn_008) and Michael addition (rxn_009) are chemically sound but lack explicit mineral catalyst specification in the Surface environment. The core cyanosulfidic steps (rxn_010–014) are strongly supported by Patel et al. (2015). Environmental flow Hydrothermal → Surface → (Biochemical via P10) is well-justified.
suggestions: Specify mineral catalysts for rxn_005 (e.g., TiO₂ or boron minerals) and rxn_008 (e.g., clay or zeolite surfaces) in the Surface environment
Provide experimental yield data or citation for rxn_002 (formate reduction to formaldehyde on magnetite)
Consider alternative formaldehyde sources (e.g., Cannizzaro disproportionation or UV photoreduction of CO₂) to strengthen upstream pathway

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Alternative route using atmospheric photochemical HCN (N₂ + CH₄ → HCN) instead of formamide decomposition. The atmospheric HCN source is well-documented but rxn_007 is labeled as 'Surface' when it is more accurately an atmospheric process that delivers HCN to surface pools. This pathway provides important redundancy for HCN supply. Shares the same upstream hydrothermal feedstock weaknesses as P1 (rxn_002). The cyanosulfidic core is identical and well-validated.
suggestions: Relabel rxn_007 as 'Atmospheric' or note it is atmospheric-to-surface transfer
Clarify the environmental transition for HCN rainout from atmosphere to surface pools

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Partial pathway terminating at the cyanohydrin step (rxn_011 producing 3-hydroxypyrrolidine-2-thione). As a branch pathway, it correctly identifies the formamide-derived HCN route to the cyanohydrin intermediate. However, it does not complete the synthesis to proline and serves only as a sub-network fragment. Useful for understanding feedstock convergence but not a standalone route to target.
suggestions: This pathway is valid as a branch; ensure it is clearly marked as a sub-pathway rather than a complete route

pathway_evaluations.feedback: The core cyanosulfidic proline pathway directly replicating Patel et al. (2015, Nature Chemistry). All five steps are experimentally demonstrated: (1) Strecker-type cyanohydrin formation, (2) H₂S-mediated thiolation-cyclization, (3) Cu/H₂S reductive deoxygenation, (4) thione-to-nitrile exchange, (5) nitrile hydrolysis. The chemistry is highly plausible and well-cited. The only minor concern is that all steps are Surface environment, which is accurate per the literature but environmental progression score is slightly lower since there is no multi-environment flow within the pathway itself. The pathway correctly produces racemic proline.
suggestions: Consider noting that nitrile hydrolysis (rxn_014) may be slow at ambient temperature and could benefit from metal ion catalysis
Acknowledge the racemic nature explicitly in the target node

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Very short branch pathway (3 steps) supplying atmospheric HCN to the thione-to-nitrile exchange step (rxn_013). As a sub-pathway it is valid — it captures the important detail that HCN is needed at two different points in the cyanosulfidic core (Steps 1 and 4), and the atmospheric source provides the second equivalent. However, this is not a complete route to proline.
suggestions: Clearly mark as a convergent branch rather than an independent pathway

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Captures the catalytic H₂S recycling cycle — H₂S consumed in rxn_011 (thiolation-cyclization) and regenerated in rxn_013 (thione-to-nitrile exchange). This is a key feature of the cyanosulfidic framework highlighted in Patel et al. (2015). Well-identified as a sub-cycle but not a route to the target. The catalytic nature of H₂S is an elegant feature of the network that is properly captured here.
suggestions: Note that H₂S recycling efficiency may be limited by losses to oxidation and metal sulfide precipitation under real prebiotic conditions

pathway_evaluations.feedback: NH₃ recycling pathway: ammonia consumed in rxn_009 (Michael addition to acrolein) and regenerated in rxn_011 (cyclization) and rxn_014 (nitrile hydrolysis). Correctly captures the nitrogen budget of the network. Two NH₃ equivalents are released for each one consumed, providing a net nitrogen source for other prebiotic chemistry. Not a standalone route to target.
suggestions: Quantify the stoichiometric NH₃ balance across the full network

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Traces the formaldehyde feedstock from CO₂ through formate to formaldehyde, then through aldol condensation and Michael addition to 3-aminopropanal, before entering the cyanosulfidic core. This addresses one of the key open questions from the literature — the prebiotic availability of 3-aminopropanal. The weakest link remains rxn_002 (formate → formaldehyde). The pathway provides a complete route to proline assuming HCN and H₂S are available from other branches.
suggestions: Consider the Cannizzaro reaction as an alternative formaldehyde source from concentrated formate

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Fischer-Tropsch-type route to acetaldehyde feeding into acrolein synthesis and then the cyanosulfidic core. The reverse water-gas shift (rxn_003) on pyrite and FTT synthesis of acetaldehyde (rxn_004) are plausible hydrothermal reactions with experimental support (e.g., Cody et al., McCollom & Seewald). The pathway correctly merges acetaldehyde with formaldehyde for the aldol condensation to acrolein. Provides a complete route to proline.
suggestions: Cite specific FTT experimental references for acetaldehyde synthesis
Specify mineral catalyst for aldol condensation rxn_008

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Downstream peptide assembly pathway using proline and glycine via wet-dry cycling. This extends the network into the biochemical environment. The peptide bond formation is plausible under wet-dry cycling conditions on mineral surfaces. However, glycine (mol_022) appears as an input that is not synthesized within this network, weakening the self-containedness of the pathway. The pathway demonstrates the functional relevance of proline as a building block for prebiotic peptides with conformational significance.
suggestions: Add a glycine synthesis sub-network (e.g., Strecker synthesis from formaldehyde + HCN + NH₃) to make mol_022 an internal node
Specify the mineral surface for wet-dry cycling (e.g., montmorillonite clay)

evaluation.network_feedback: This is an excellent, well-constructed synthesis network for DL-Proline that faithfully implements the cyanosulfidic pathway of Patel et al. (2015, Nature Chemistry) as its core and embeds it within a broader prebiotic feedstock-generation framework. Key strengths: (1) The core 5-step cyanosulfidic pathway (P4) is experimentally validated and scores very highly (0.93). (2) The network topology is rich with 23 molecules, 15 reactions, 4 well-connected hubs (HCN, NH₃, H₂S, formate), and 4 convergence points. (3) Environmental flow from Hydrothermal (feedstock generation) → Surface (cyanosulfidic chemistry) → Biochemical (peptide assembly) is clearly established and well-justified. (4) The catalytic H₂S and NH₃ recycling cycles are correctly captured. (5) Two independent HCN sources (formamide decomposition and atmospheric photochemistry) provide convergent feedstock supply. (6) Literature alignment is outstanding — the network directly implements the Patel et al. pathway with correct intermediates, agents, and conditions. Minor weaknesses: (a) rxn_002 (formate → formaldehyde) has limited experimental support. (b) Some Surface reactions (rxn_005, rxn_008) lack explicit mineral catalyst specification. (c) rxn_007 is mislabeled as Surface when it is atmospheric photochemistry. (d) Glycine is introduced in rxn_015 without being synthesized within the network. (e) The network correctly notes the racemic product but could more explicitly address the chirality question. Overall, this network demonstrates sophisticated understanding of prebiotic chemistry, strong literature grounding, and sound network design principles.
